title: It's official: Germany are no longer a soccer superpower - ESPN
original_text:
Defenders: Sergiño Dest, Chris Richards, Antonee Robinson, Auston Trusty, Miles Robinson, Tim Ream, Alex Freeman, Max Arfsten, Mark McKenzie, Joe Scally
Midfielders: Tyler Adams, Weston McKennie, Brendan Aaronson, Sebastian Berhalter, Cristian Roldan, Malik Tillman, Alejandro Zendejas
Forwards: Gio Reyna, Ricardo Pepi, Christian Pulisic, Haji Wright, Folarin Balogun, Tim Weah
117
81
By
Ryan Rosenblatt
26/05/2026 04:20 pmMay 26, 2026 16:20 EDT
Thanks for joining us
The Americans have their World Cup roster!
The 26 players who will rep the red, white and blue on home soil for the most anticipated tournament in the country's soccer history have been chosen, and kickoff is right around the corner.
Thanks for joining us for today's roster reveal, and make sure you stick with ESPNFC for all the reaction from the squad, as well as what Pochettino has to say about his process for picking the team.
Until next time, have a great day.
17
5
By
Ryan Rosenblatt
26/05/2026 04:18 pmMay 26, 2026 16:18 EDT
The upcoming friendlies could be crucial for the defense
Pochettino taking 10 defenders to the World Cup seems to indicate that he doesn't have total confidence in the options available to him. Yes, he's likely to play with five at the back, so that necessitates more defenders on the roster, but that still doesn't explain all the choices.
Keep an eye on the two friendlies the USMNT has before the World Cup. Who plays, in what roles and, mostly, who performs well, could decide which players are on the field at the World Cup.
All those defenders could be a sign that Pochettino wants to continue figuring things out in these last couple of weeks, so while we do have a roster, there are still decisions left to make.
12
0
By
Ryan Rosenblatt
26/05/2026 04:15 pmMay 26, 2026 16:15 EDT
Welcome back, Sergiño Dest
There was a time when Sergiño Dest was hailed as a vital player to the U.S., but he has faded into the background of late, largely due to injuries. He suffered a knee ligament tear in spring 2024 that kept him out nearly a year and then was sidelined again this spring because of a hamstring injury, having returned earlier this month.
But when Dest is at his best, he's a dynamic force from the fullback spot and has a technical ability that few on the U.S. team offer. While the Americans might be short on midfielders and truly creative passers, Dest brings that, even if it's a little more unusual to get it from a fullback.
With Dest healthy again and back in the team, could he be the magic man to help unlock the U.S. attack?
20
3
By
Ryan Rosenblatt
26/05/2026 04:12 pmMay 26, 2026 16:12 EDT
You could see the U.S. feed off the excitement today
The American players really seemed to relish the excitement in New York as fans cheered them on stage. This is a World Cup on home soil, something these players are lucky to experience this one time, and it appears as if they are really soaking it in.
The excitement in the stadiums this summer should be something to witness, and it could be those home crowds pushing the U.S. through in difficult games. The players sure seem to be relishing the opportunity.
15
3
By
Ryan Rosenblatt
26/05/2026 04:10 pmMay 26, 2026 16:10 EDT
Haji Wright could play a sneakily important role
Haji Wright is rarely tabbed as one of the most important USMNT players, but he could play an important role at the World Cup.
He has proven himself internationally before, with seven goals in 20 caps. They're important goals, too, having netted against Netherlands at the last World Cup and scored two in extra time to beat Jamaica in the CONCACAF Nations League semifinals. He has also scored twice in a friendly against Australia, one of the Americans' group stage opponents.
The Coventry striker had a good year in the Championship, scoring 17 times, so form is not a worry and, maybe most importantly, he brings a physicality the U.S. could use.
Paraguay will force the Americans to play in the air, and Australia could as well. Having a front man with a big body who is comfortable dealing with physical play could be massive for the U.S. at this World Cup, and Wright is their best bet to deliver on that.

Biggest USMNT roster worry
Lack of midfielders
Starting goalkeeper
Who starts at striker
By
Ryan Rosenblatt
26/05/2026 04:05 pmMay 26, 2026 16:05 EDT
Some important roster dates
While the U.S. did announce its roster today, it does not have to submit it to FIFA until June 1. If anyone suffers an injury, an injured player makes a shockingly quick recovery or Pochettino just decides to make a change, the American squad can change in the next week.
Also, teams have until 24 hours before their first match to make a roster change in the case of an injury, so further changes are still possible next month.

First-tier talent
For the first time in the modern era (since 1990), every player on the United States men's World Cup squad plays on a club in the top flight in their country (based on team's league in the upcoming season).

Who is the rising star of this roster?
It has to be Alex Freeman. From still earning minutes in reserve MLS games a couple of years ago to now getting appearances in La Liga. He could play as either a wing-back or possibly even an RCB in this XI.

USA's 2026 World Cup roster reveal recap: Pulisic, Reyna headline 26-man squad
ESPN
May 26, 2026, 04:24 PM ET
On Tuesday, we found out which 26 players were called upon by manager Mauricio Pochettino to represent the United States national team on home soil at the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
Enjoy all that happened from New York City with special analysis from our reporters and experts as they reacted to Pochettino's list for the World Cup.
play
Leboeuf: Germany should sack Nagelsmann after Paraguay defeat (2:32)
Rob DawsonJun 29, 2026, 09:31 PM ET
FOXBOROUGH, Mass. -- This was a era-defining result. Playing in their first FIFA World Cup knockout game in 12 years, four-time winners Germany were beaten by Paraguay on penalties Monday after a stale 1-1 draw at Boston Stadium.
Germany have never before lost a World Cup penalty shootout, but this is a shadow of the teams from their illustrious past. The players from 1954, 1974, 1990 and 2014 would have found a way to win against a Paraguay team who were thrashed 4-1 by the United States in their opening group game and only reached the last 32 as the seventh of eight third-place qualifiers.
That Germany arrived here in the conversation as possible winners was only because of what they've achieved before. Julian Nagelsmann's version of Die Mannschaft is a pale imitation.
The truth is that even had they scraped through, their World Cup would have ended abruptly against France in the next round anyway. Maybe the shock of losing to Paraguay will be enough to jolt a reaction out of a once-famous national team that has endured a miserable decade at this tournament following group-stage exits in 2018 and 2022.
"If you exit after the first stage, it's not enough for German football," Nagelsmann said. "This is now the third elimination in a row, so we are not part of the first-class teams any more."
While Germany contemplate one of their worst World Cup results, Paraguay enjoyed perhaps their best. And whatever happens to the South Americans for the rest of this tournament, they will always have Boston.
Paraguay had never scored a World Cup knockout goal before facing Germany on Monday. Not only did Julio Enciso do that but Paraguay followed it up with a determined, dogged defensive display to reach the last 16. It was fitting that José Canale scored the winning spot kick after spending most of the second half and extra time throwing himself in front of shot after shot.
When the final penalty hit the net, there were tears in the stands from fans wearing red and white. Germany, who arrived with high expectations, slumped to their knees in the center circle.
The postmortem after this tournament will not be kind to Nagelsmann's side.
"Probably we need to do a big change, we will see," said the former Bayern Munich coach. "We need some solutions for the future. I won't go into details today but there are some things that need to be changed."
Paraguay, on the other hand, have already won.
Maybe it's Kylian Mbappé and France next, but it doesn't matter. A team that beat Brazil and Argentina in qualifying have added another historic result to their CV. Paraguay fans will never forget where they were when they beat Germany at the World Cup.
While Paraguay move on, the fallout for Germany begins now.
No one scored more than their 10 goals in the first round, and they finished top of Group E, but there was still a sense that this was coming. There were worrying spells in all three group games against Curaçao, Ivory Coast and particularly in the defeat to Ecuador.
Nagelsmann argued that Germany had enough chances to beat Paraguay and branded the decision to rule out Jonathan Tah's header in extra time after a VAR check "a joke," but this was no smash and grab. Paraguay deservedly led at halftime and largely kept Germany in check, aside from Kai Havertz's equalizer.
There was criticism of Nagelsmann and his players before this game, and there is more on the way. Much more.
The first half in particular was dire.
At his news conference Sunday, Nagelsmann described Paraguay as "an uncomfortable opponent," and that's exactly what they were. Gustavo Alfaro's team defended deep and stayed compact. Every stoppage was dragged out for as long as possible.
Germany struggled to cope with the lack of flow, and it was Paraguay who made the most of their rare forays forward. The first was after just 30 seconds, and Manuel Neuer -- whose place on the team has been repeatedly questioned at home -- was forced into a save from Júnior Alonso.
Paraguay barely crossed the halfway line again until just before halftime. Matías Galarza's clever overlapping run was followed by a whipped cross into the box, which Enciso headed in.
One of the worries for this Germany team before the tournament was their defending, and they failed to keep a clean sheet in any of their four games. At the other end, their 0.14 expected goals in the first half was their second lowest in a World Cup knockout game in the past 60 years -- all despite having nearly 80% possession.
The pace of Germany's attacking in the first 45 minutes suggested they were struggling to deal with Foxborough's midafternoon heat. It picked up in the second half, when Joshua Kimmich moved into midfield, but Paraguay were never stretched to a breaking point. Frustration had already begun to seep in long before the penalties began.
And where historically the Germans are near faultless from the spot, there was a lack of confidence from some of their takers. Havertz and Nick Woltemade both had their efforts saved by Orlando Gill.
Paraguay missed two chances to seal the win, but when Tah stepped up with the chance to shift momentum back toward Germany, he skied his kick into the Boston sunset. Canale, outstanding all game, made no mistake with Paraguay's third chance to win.
Nagelsmann was quickly back out to face the music.
"Will you continue as coach?" came the inevitable question. "I would like to," he said before walking away.
It won't be the last time he faces questions about his future in the days and weeks to come. Germany couldn't find any answers against Paraguay, and now the autopsy begins.
